blocks.content: Cassandra walked Ethan to the porch.

The night was warm and the street was quiet — the particular quiet of a neighborhood in late evening. One dog somewhere. A television behind a lit window. The sound of the world settling in. She stood on the top step, which put her almost at his height, and they faced each other in the spill of the porch light.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: They liked you. My dad especially.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: How can you tell?

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: He said good twice. That's his version of a standing ovation.
He smiled in the specific way she'd come to know — the one that moved through his whole face rather than just his mouth.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: Can I ask you something?

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: Yeah.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: There's something in the air. I don't know what it is — I'm not asking you to name it, and I'm not going to push. But I've been feeling it for a while. I just want you to know that I notice. And noticing isn't the same as needing. I'll wait for whatever you want to say when you want to say it.
She looked at him for a long moment. At the face she'd been studying for weeks and was still learning.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: Whatever it is you're not ready to say yet — I'm not going anywhere.
She didn't answer right away. She looked at the street — the familiar block, the quiet she'd walked a thousand times.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: I know.
A pause.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: That's what makes it harder.
He didn't understand that. She could feel that he didn't — the way you feel the edge of a thing you can't yet see. He didn't ask what she meant. He just nodded.
He kissed her — brief, right — and walked down the steps into the evening.
She watched him go until he turned the corner. He didn't look back. She appreciated that about him — that he trusted her to still be there without needing to confirm it.
She went inside. Her father was on the back porch with a glass of water and the stillness of a man thinking. She went out and stood beside him.

blocks.speaker: Father
blocks.content: He'll handle it.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: You think?

blocks.speaker: Father
blocks.content: I do. He has the specific quality that matters most. Not strength — that can be performed. He has honesty. The kind that doesn't run from its own uncertainty.
She leaned against him, the way she had since she was a child. He put his arm around her. They stood in the evening quiet and she breathed, and that was enough.
On the other side of town, Ethan walked home with his sketchbook under his arm and her words moving through him.
I know. That's what makes it harder.

He turned it over. Looked at it from different angles, the way he looked at paintings that weren't quite done — trying to find the part he was missing. He didn't find it yet. But he kept it. Filed it somewhere careful, the way he filed things that would mean more later.
He let himself into his apartment. The lamp on. Her canvas still on the table.
He sat down across from it. He opened his sketchbook to a clean page. He began to draw.
